Name: latency_optimized_inference
Title: Latency-Optimized Inference for Large Language Models on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging model quantization, optimized memory management, and speculative decoding, we can significantly reduce the latency of large language model inference on commodity GPUs without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and Compiling Code LLMs into Lightweight Executables have demonstrated the potential for optimizing language model inference on commodity GPUs. Our proposal builds upon these ideas by exploring the intersection of model quantization, memory management, and speculative decoding to achieve latency-optimized inference. Unlike Spectral Latent Steering, which focuses on test-time optimization, our approach prioritizes latency reduction through hardware-aware optimizations.
Abstract: Large language models are increasingly being deployed on commodity GPUs, but their inference latency remains a significant bottleneck. To address this, we propose a latency-optimized inference framework that combines model quantization, optimized memory management, and speculative decoding. Our approach achieves a significant reduction in latency while maintaining accuracy, making it suitable for real-time applications. We evaluate our framework on a range of benchmarks, including LLaMA-7B, and demonstrate its effectiveness in reducing latency and improving throughput.
Experiments: Evaluate the effect of model quantization on latency and accuracy using LLaMA-7B as a benchmark.
Investigate the impact of optimized memory management on latency and throughput using a range of memory-constrained scenarios.
Assess the benefits of speculative decoding in reducing latency and improving accuracy, and explore the trade-offs between different decoding strategies.
Conduct a comprehensive evaluation of our latency-optimized inference framework on a range of benchmarks, including LLaMA-7B, and compare its performance to existing state-of-the-art methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may not be effective for all types of language models or applications, and may require significant tuning and optimization for specific use cases.
The use of model quantization and speculative decoding may introduce additional computational overhead, which could negatively impact latency and throughput.
The framework's reliance on optimized memory management may limit its applicability to memory-constrained devices or scenarios.
